\xf0\x9f\x92\xa5More Rite Aid Drama!\xf0\x9f\x92\xa5

Goddamn it.

Every time we think we\xe2\x80\x99re close to being done with Rite Aid Corp, it just keeps rearing its ugly head. We literally covered its confirmation a week ago\xe2\x80\xa6*

\xf0\x9f\x92\xa5Rite-Aid Lives to See Another Day.\xf0\x9f\x92\xa5

Jul 3
Read full story

\xe2\x80\xa6 but once again there are issues.

And not even particularly original issues.

Remember MedImpact Healthcare Systems Inc. (\xe2\x80\x9cMedImpact\xe2\x80\x9d)? It\xe2\x80\x99s back! We last discussed these guys two weeks ago in the run up to the confirmation hearing:

\xf0\x9f\x92\xa5A Haircut Coming? Part II.\xf0\x9f\x92\xa5

Jun 26
Read full story

MedImpact disputed ~$225mm of assumed liabilities associated with the debtors\xe2\x80\x99 Elixir sale. The debtors thought MedImpact\xe2\x80\x99s position was ridiculous and Judge Kaplan agreed. During a June 24, 2024 hearing, Judge Kaplan ordered MedImpact to perform under the original asset purchase agreement and sale order.

By the time of the confirmation hearing on June 27, 2024, MedImpact had already appealed in district court, and, apparently, also refused to actually perform and pay a liability due to McKesson Corp ($MCK).**

So the debtors, obviously a bit concerned at the lack of cooperation, filed a motion seeking an order holding MedImpact in civil contempt. LOL!

Per the motion:

\xe2\x80\x9cMedImpact has not complied with the Sale Order or Enforcement Order. On July 1 and July 3, the Debtors specifically gave MedImpact two chances to confirm that MedImpact will pay one of the six Disputed Liability Types that were teed up in the First Motion to Enforce: the $35 million \xe2\x80\x9cMcKesson Liabilities\xe2\x80\x9d that have been due since Closing and that MedImpact must satisfy for the now confirmed Plan to go effective.\xe2\x80\x9d (emphasis added)

But MedImpact apparently indicated to the debtors that they had \xe2\x80\x9cno information on the composition of this alleged liability to McKesson.\xe2\x80\x9d MedImpact be like \xe2\x80\x9ccompel\xe2\x80\x9d\xe2\x80\xa6

The debtors aren\xe2\x80\x99t buying it. In fact, they highlighted how (i) during the previous dispute, one of MedImpact\xe2\x80\x99s witnesses mentioned the liability, (ii) the liability is on the closing working capital list verified by MedImpact, (iii) the invoice is on Elixir\xe2\x80\x99s subledger which MedImpact controls, and (iv) the individual invoices were listed in a file that MedImpact sent to the debtors back in May. In other words, per the debtors:

\xe2\x80\x9cMedImpact knows exactly what the McKesson Liabilities are, why they must be paid, how much must be paid, and when they were due to be paid by MedImpact.\xe2\x80\x9d

\xe2\x80\x9cMedImpact had its day in Court and lost. MedImpact\xe2\x80\x99s continued play for self-help must end. MedImpact is slow walking payment\xe2\x80\x94perhaps in an effort to use the Debtors\xe2\x80\x99 tight liquidity situation to try to renegotiate aspects of the Court\xe2\x80\x99s orders. But anything short of full compliance is unacceptable to the Debtors and a disservice to their customers, creditors, and employees. MedImpact knows full well the import of its actions and who will be harmed by them.\xe2\x80\x9d

After all the debtors have gone through in this case, they\xe2\x80\x99ve got to be feeling like this:

We know we are. And we reckon Judge Kaplan is too. Something tells us this won\xe2\x80\x99t bode well for MedImpact.

A hearing is set for July 29, 2024 at 11am ET.

*You can refer to our other prior RAD coverage here, here, here, here, here, and here.
**To be clear there also hasn\xe2\x80\x99t been any proposed stay pending appeal from MedImpact.

Thank you for reading PETITION. This post is public so feel free to share it.

\xf0\x9f\x92\xa5New Chapter 11 Bankruptcy - Basic Fun Inc.\xf0\x9f\x92\xa5

Damn it! Can\xe2\x80\x99t we at least have our f*cking toys!!

On June 28, 2024, FL-based Basic Fun Inc. (\xe2\x80\x9cBasic Fun\xe2\x80\x9d) and four affiliates (collectively, the \xe2\x80\x9cdebtors\xe2\x80\x9d or the \xe2\x80\x9ccompany\xe2\x80\x9d) filed chapter 11 bankruptcy cases in the District of Delaware (Judge Goldblatt). The debtors are purveyors of fun \xe2\x80\x94 a \xe2\x80\x9cglobal children\xe2\x80\x99s entertainment company,\xe2\x80\x9d founded in 2009 by Jay Foreman.

Jay\xe2\x80\x99s got a passion for toys and a penchant for horrible TikToks like this \xe2\xac\x87\xef\xb8\x8f one:

\xe2\x80\x9cMeet Frank\xe2\x80\x9d\xe2\x80\xa6 the CFO of a toy company \xe2\x80\xa6 really, guys?!? With \xe2\x80\x9ccontent\xe2\x80\x9d like this, it\xe2\x80\x99s no wonder you\xe2\x80\x99re bankrupt. (The post got 26 \xe2\x80\x9clikes,\xe2\x80\x9d lol).

Since \xe2\x80\x9813, Jay has been busy rolling up toy brands. \xe2\x80\x9cWell-known, steady\xe2\x80\x9d toys please, the better to build a \xe2\x80\x9cconsistent profit base,\xe2\x80\x9d according to the first-day declaration of \xe2\x80\xa6 wait for it \xe2\x80\xa6 CFO Frank!* At least eight toybox-stuffing transactions since \xe2\x80\x9813, including M&A, 363 purchases and licensing deals.** And why not? Tots are always going to love Lincoln Logs. And Tonka Trucks. Tinker Toys! Care Bears! Lite-Brite! With all new templates! My Little Pony! Johnny\xe2\x80\x99s aged grandpa remembers them all: back in the day, he says, before you kids with your smartphones, \xe2\x80\x9cfun\xe2\x80\x9d was lobbing firecrackers at Lincoln Log cities and reveling in the conflagration. Is there any asset more capable of delivering annuity-like cash flows than hoary toys for nostalgic boomers? \xf0\x9f\x99\x84

Much to our surprise, apparently nostalgia \xe2\x80\x94 the success of the Barbie movie notwithstanding \xe2\x80\x94 isn\xe2\x80\x99t a guarantee of business success. Per our boy Frank-of-26-Likes:

\xe2\x80\x9cDespite the Company\xe2\x80\x99s successful acquisition of many major toy companies and products over 10 years, in fiscal year 2023, the Company recognized total net sales of approximately $144.1 million, representing a decrease of $15.3 million, or 9.6%, compared to fiscal year 2022.\xe2\x80\x9d (emphasis added)

How do we define success these days, \xf0\x9f\xa4\x94? Sales only down 9.6%, \xf0\x9f\xa4\x94? So Time Warner\xe2\x80\x99s buyout of America Online was a successful exercise of sound business judgment?

To be fair, the debtors suffered from more than their share of exogenous hits. The Toys R Us (\xe2\x80\x9cTRU\xe2\x80\x9d) bankruptcy, for instance: TRU was the debtors\xe2\x80\x99 largest customer, with some $35mm of annual sales. TRU\xe2\x80\x99s chapter 11 filing and liquidation left the debtors with $6mm in uncollected receivables; they also faced \xe2\x80\x9cstruggles\xe2\x80\x9d to shift the TRU volume to Amazon Inc. ($AMZN). Hmmm, maybe they should have partnered with Thrasio Holdings LLC, \xf0\x9f\x98\x9c?

And, of course, Covid-19. Demand from stay-at-homes surged, but the good news was shot through with shards of awfulness. As \xe2\x80\x9ca result of a shortage in container boxes and ships, the goods being produced were not being shipped on time to meet demand.\xe2\x80\x9d And when they were shipped, costs were some seven to ten times higher than before. At the same time, amusement parks were shuttered by social distancing mandates; the debtors\xe2\x80\x99 amusement division lost $10mm in sales in \xe2\x80\x9820. But when the restrictions lifted, consumers fat with stimulus money decided to blow it on (other) \xe2\x80\x9cexperiences\xe2\x80\x9d like travel and dining. This came as the supply chain issues were resolved; suddenly the debtors were loaded with excess inventory, meaning margin-busting markdowns and persistently inflated shipping costs.

You\xe2\x80\x99ve heard variations on this for quite some time, have you not? All excruciatingly, painfully familiar, is it not? Bid it farewell as we venture into the unknown of the debtors\xe2\x80\x99 capital structure. First, Frank whipped up this nifty org chart:***

BF GP Inc., a Delaware corporation, is the general partner of Basic Fun LP, the non-debtor parent. Our boy Jay owns 100% of BF GP Inc. The debt sits below.

Let\xe2\x80\x99s get into that debt shall we? Don\xe2\x80\x99t let those TikToks fool you: we reckon that Frank was thinking \xe2\x80\x9cwhy the f*ck am I making TikToks, Jay, when I\xe2\x80\x99ve got a bunch of liabilities on balance sheet to contend with?\xe2\x80\x9d A fair sentiment, we think.

On Aug. 3, 2017, the debtors obtained $40mm in senior secured loans, $15mm in mezzanine debt and access to a $20mm accordion via Royal Bank of Canada (\xe2\x80\x9cRBC\xe2\x80\x9d) as part of the debtors\xe2\x80\x99 acquisition of Tech 4 Kids. The mezzanine facility has a PIK component. Frank-of-26-likes-and-tens-of-millions-of-debt dubs these the \xe2\x80\x9cInitial Loans.\xe2\x80\x9d In December of that year, the senior lenders advanced $5mm through the accordion to support the debtors\xe2\x80\x99 acquisition of K\xe2\x80\x99Nex; the advance was matched by a former investor in K\xe2\x80\x99Nex, CCNAS Investment Holdings, Jay and John MacDonald, a member of the board and presumably Jay\xe2\x80\x99s buddy.

\xe2\x80\x9cGrowth\xe2\x80\x9d on their minds, meaning new acquisitions, the company sought a private equity partner to \xe2\x80\x9cinvest in the business.\xe2\x80\x9d Jay engaged BMO Capital Markets to assist. \xe2\x80\x9cThough there were initially several interested parties, due to factors including the Toys R Us Bankruptcy . . . only one party, Falcon Structured Equity Partners, LP (\xe2\x80\x9cFalcon\xe2\x80\x9d), committed to an investment that the Non-Debtor Parent and its existing equity holders deemed to be acceptable.\xe2\x80\x9d Falcon invested $40mm, for which it got 400k units of 10% Series C Preferred Partnership Interests and 178,533.48 Class A-4 Limited Partnership Interests, which together represent 14% of the \xe2\x80\x9ccommon equity\xe2\x80\x9d of the non-debtor parent.

And what did the company do with that $40mm? Well, they first loaned the debtors $10mm on a subordinated basis. As for the rest, well, there was that $5mm match to contend with:

The Non-Debtor Parent had borrowed the $5,000,000 from Messrs. Foreman and MacDonald pursuant to notes issued by Non-Debtor Parent, which notes to Messrs. Foreman and MacDonald were repaid by Non-Debtor Parent from the proceeds of the Falcon Investment. The K\xe2\x80\x99Nex Note is subordinated in payment to all of the current and past secured debt obligations of the Company.

But why stop there:

The remainder of the $40,000,000 equity investment repaid Foreman and MacDonald and a former unit holder $17,000,000 for his 26% interest in the company, senior employee distributions and transaction fees.

A lot ended up with our boy Jay and his boy MacDonald, unless we misread it somehow. \xe2\x80\x9cThe anticipated benefits of a business partner with $3 billion of assets to deploy to grow the Company have not materialized,\xe2\x80\x9d Frank says. Oh? \xe2\x80\x9cLetters of intent requiring equity were not available and additional equity contributions from Foreman and MacDonald could not be invested with priority to the Falcon Investment.\xe2\x80\x9d Huh? Bit of a head-scratcher, but per Frank, there hasn\xe2\x80\x99t been much on the M&A front since \xe2\x80\x9818 due to the constraints the Falcon capital put on the company.

By \xe2\x80\x9819, events spiraled somewhat out of the company\xe2\x80\x99s control. RBC, rattled by the TRU bankruptcy and the pandemic, took a look at the Sept. \xe2\x80\x9920 maturity of that two-year old $20mm senior secured term and told the company that there was absolutely no way it would extend one second longer. \xe2\x80\x9cRBC moved the account to their Special Loan\xe2\x80\x99s Group and hired A&M to monitor cashflow and report on the company\xe2\x80\x99s performance. A Term Sheet negotiated with PNC was ultimately withdrawn as a result of Covid-19 impact uncertainty.\xe2\x80\x9d Certainly not an ideal time to hit the debt capital markets, but capital availability is often a function of price and so in marched Crystal Financial, d/b/a SLR Credit Solutions (\xe2\x80\x9cSLR\xe2\x80\x9d) with what Frank-of-26-likes-and-26-private-credit-battle-scars refers to, somewhat ominously, as The 2020 Refinancing Transaction.

SLR provided a $20mm revolver, subject to a traditional ABL borrowing base, and a $20mm term loan. RBC got some relief. As of the petition date, the debtors owed \xe2\x80\x9cnot less than $20,000,000\xe2\x80\x9d to SLR with respect to the term loan, and $326,164.74 on the revolver.

RBC still had mezzanine piece. On July 15, 2020, RBC loaned the company another $2.5mm from the facility. Our boy Jay and his boy MacDonald promptly purchased \xe2\x80\x9c100% participation interest in RBC\xe2\x80\x99s right, title and interest\xe2\x80\x9d in and to that loan (MacMahon calls it the \xe2\x80\x9cFirst Out Loan\xe2\x80\x9d). And if that\xe2\x80\x99s not enough, on Oct. 20 of the same year, the mezz lenders advanced another $2.5mm to the company. Frank says it\xe2\x80\x99s been repaid in full. Outstanding principal on the mezzanine piece is \xe2\x80\x9cnot less than $[16,436,335.00]\xe2\x80\x9d as of the petition date.

Our boy Jay and his boy McDonald themselves loaned the company a total of $18mm in \xe2\x80\x9919. Frank says that the company \xe2\x80\x9cneeded liquidity\xe2\x80\x9d as it was in talks with a jumpy RBC \xe2\x80\x9cto obtain forbearance from certain defaults pursuant to the 2017 RBC Senior Credit Agreement and the 2017 Mezzanine Credit Agreement.\xe2\x80\x9d (We\xe2\x80\x99ll get to that.)

What\xe2\x80\x99s left? $900k owed to shippers and warehousemen**** and $11.6mm in unsecured obligations to suppliers, vendors and the like. In terms of other major obligations, the debtors have their HQ lease in Boca, their storage lease in Boca, and showrooms in LA and Hong Kong.

The 2020 Refinancing Transaction with SLR bought some time. But in Oct. \xe2\x80\x9923, trailing twelve-month financials fell below levels required by the SLR facility. The company\xe2\x80\x99s forecasts for \xe2\x80\x9924 looked worse. In December \xe2\x80\x9923, Frank-of-26-likes-and-26-sh*t-bricks-in-his-pants says, the \xe2\x80\x9cdefault\xe2\x80\x9d under the 2020 credit agreement with SLR caused \xe2\x80\x9c(i) the Company to accrue contractual fees and default interest to SLR, (ii) the amortization under the First Out Loans to end after only one month, and (iii) interest payments to RBC to end, resulting in a PIK 18% rate.\xe2\x80\x9d

These credit agreement defaults were still outstanding in June \xe2\x80\x9924. SLR chose not to exercise its right to implement cash dominion over the company\xe2\x80\x99s accounts. However, the springing covenant clause meant that the facility matured on July 2 or 90 days prior to the Sept. 30 maturity of the mezz piece. The company asked for a revolver advance. Nope, SLR said. The company \xe2\x80\x9c\xe2\x80\xa6voluntarily complied with SLR\xe2\x80\x99s request to transfer daily receipts to SLR\xe2\x80\x99s collection account, with funds being re-advanced to the Company upon request.\xe2\x80\x9d

Back to the mezz piece. The company is in a covenant-based default. And, because we know Frank like we do (thanks TikTok!), we reckon he found RBC relentlessly annoying:

\xe2\x80\x9cAs a condition to renewing the mezzanine loans, RBC signaled its desire to see (i) the Company\xe2\x80\x99s access to capital to support operations, including through an acquisition strategy as a means to grow EBITDA, and (ii) re- alignment of management and equity interests in order to ensure that management was properly incentivized, and their interests were aligned with those of the Company.\xe2\x80\x9d

So RBC hadn\xe2\x80\x99t thought to bring sort of thing up before? Then there\xe2\x80\x99s this odd nugget:

In April 2024, the Company\xe2\x80\x99s CFO and John MacDonald, the Chair of the Debtors\xe2\x80\x99 governing boards (the \xe2\x80\x9cBoards\xe2\x80\x9d), held rounds of discussion with SLR, RBC, and Falcon. Following multiple calls with RBC and Falcon, the Company received a response from Falcon that did not adequately provide for access to capital or management alignment. RBC recommended that the Company retain an investment banker to provide valuation and strategic assistance in moving past the impasse with SLR, RBC, and Falcon.

The company says it urgently needs \xe2\x80\x9cliquidity\xe2\x80\x9d to \xe2\x80\x9cbegin making acquisitions as a path to growing EBITDA.\xe2\x80\x9d SLR and RBC seem to be taking a dim view of the company\xe2\x80\x99s ability to do that very thing. Who loses money on Lincoln Logs? At any rate, the company retained Oppenheimer & Co. (\xe2\x80\x9cOppenheimer\xe2\x80\x9d) as investment banker; Oppenheimer presented two options and in June the company chose Great Rock Capital Partners (\xe2\x80\x9cGreat Rock\xe2\x80\x9d). Great Rock sent its people to Florida and \xe2\x80\x9ccommenced its borrowing base audit. At the same time, Oppenheimer had further conversations with RBC, SLR, and Falcon \xe2\x80\xa6 Also in June 2024, SLR tightened oversight over the Company. Disbursements were strictly limited to deposits which is constraining during June, a busy month in the cycle of a toy company.\xe2\x80\x9d

June may be busy, but July will be memorable. At the first day hearing on July 2, 2024, the debtors\xe2\x80\x99 motions were approved on an interim basis, including a $22.5mm out of a proposed $50mm DIP RCF from Great Rock. Judge Goldblatt also approved a $10mm DIP term facility as a roll-up of RBC\xe2\x80\x99s mezz piece and a $5mm junior secured delay draw term loan from our boy Jay and his boy John. The RBC roll-up was a requirement to priming. SLR\xe2\x80\x99s debt remains senior to RBC until paid off (which is contemplated).

The first day hearing was surprisingly congenial (and short, clocking in just over an hour), given the intimations of strife in the filings. Mark Joachim of Polsinelli PC, counsel for the debtors, stated that the parties to the cases, including RBC, SLR and Falcon, have worked \xe2\x80\x9cfeverishly\xe2\x80\x9d to come to an agreement on a \xe2\x80\x9cfully consensual debt financing,\xe2\x80\x9d but a \xe2\x80\x9crestructuring of the overall capital structure\xe2\x80\x9d is still in process as the parties put \xe2\x80\x9cthe finishing touches\xe2\x80\x9d on. But an \xe2\x80\x9cagreement in principle\xe2\x80\x9d is close!

For its part, Falcon, noting that the company is founder-owned and subject to \xe2\x80\x9ccorporate darkness,\xe2\x80\x9d insisted on the appointment of a \xe2\x80\x9cmutually acceptable\xe2\x80\x9d independent director familiar with restructuring. There may yet be some drama!

The debtors are represented by the aforementioned Polsinelli (Shanti Katona, Mark Joachim, Caryn Wang, Tanya Behnam, Michael Schuster) as counsel \xe2\x80\x94 part of a nice string of cases including ProSomnus Inc. and Delta Apparel Inc. \xe2\x80\x94 and Oppenheimer as financial advisor (Bruce Buchanan, Matthew Haasch, Geoffrey Weiss, Isaac Weinstock). RBC is represented by Orrick, Herrington & Sutcliffe LLP (Raniero D\xe2\x80\x99Aversa, Nicholas Sabatino) and Klehr Harrison Harvey Branzburg LLP (Domenic Pacitti, Richard Beck, Sally Veghte), SLR by Morgan Lewis & Bockius LLP (Christopher Carter, Sandra Vrejan, David Shim, Jody Barillare), Falcon by Paul Weiss Rifkind Wharton & Garrison LLP (Paul Basta, Sean Mitchell, Paul Paterson, Nicholas Krislov) and Landis Rath & Cobb LLP (Richard Cobb, Joshua Brooks), and Great Rock by Paul Hastings LLP (Justin Rawlins, Matthew Friedrick, Xue Yu, Jennifer Hildebrandt) and Young Conaway Stargatt & Taylor LLP (Edmon Morton, Matthew Lunn).

*One name only for this legendary CFO. Okay, fine, McMahon is his last name.
**The company owns a variety of brands (e.g., K\xe2\x80\x99nex, Mash\xe2\x80\x99ems, Misfittens, Cutetitos, Playhut, and Uncle Milton) acquired over the aforementioned 10-year acquisition bonanza and licenses others (e.g., \xe2\x80\x9ciconic brands\xe2\x80\x9d like Care Bears, Lite Brite, Tonka, Nintendo Amusement, Lincoln Logs, and My Little Pony Retro), the latter making up ~70% of the company\xe2\x80\x99s revenue. The debtors sell their toys in places like Target Inc. ($TGT), Walmart Inc. ($WMT), Macy\xe2\x80\x99s Inc. ($M), Costco Inc. ($COST), online on Amazon Inc. ($AMZN), and at amusement parks.
***What? No \xe2\x80\x9cmaking of\xe2\x80\x9d vid on TikTok, guys, c\xe2\x80\x99mon?
****Half of which may be taken care of via the interim order authorizing the debtors to pay prepetition claims of shippers, et al.
